verses.esv: Thus the heavens and the earth were finished, and all the host of them.
insights: wayḵullū (ויכלו) — and were finished — is the Pual form of the root kālāh (כלה), meaning to complete or finish. The Pual (passive intensive) indicates the heavens and earth were brought to completion. This passive completion signals a divine act received, not simply done.

ṣĕḇāʾām (צבאם) — their host — the same word used for armies and for the angelic host. The stars, planets, and all heavenly bodies are conceived as an organized army. The same word underlies the divine title YHWH Ṣĕḇāʾōṯ — LORD of Hosts.

Genesis 2:1 serves as a summary title for what follows: the creation was finished. The wayyiqtol chain of Genesis 1 — and God said, and God made, and God called — now resolves into the Pual perfect: they were finished.

verses.esv: And on the seventh day God finished his work that he had done, and he rested on the seventh day from all his work that he had done.
insights: wayyiḵal (ויכל) — and he finished — the Piel of kālāh, here with God as subject. The Piel is intensive active: God actively brought his work to its completion on the seventh day. There was a final intentional act of finishing.

šabbat (שבת) — he rested — the root of Shabbat/Sabbath. But the verb means to cease, to stop, not to be tired or recuperate. God rests not from exhaustion but as sovereign conclusion. Stopping is a creative act.

mĕlaḵtô (מלאכתו) — his work — the same root gives the word malʾāḵ (angel, messenger). God's creative work (mĕlāḵāh) is carried out by one who acts as a commissioned emissary — and the word for that work shapes the later concept of the divinely commissioned agent.

verses.esv: So God blessed the seventh day and made it holy, because on it God rested from all his work that he had done in creation.
insights: wayĕḇāreḵ (ויברך) — and he blessed — the third occurrence of the root bāraḵ in Genesis (after creatures in 1:22 and humans in 1:28). Here God blesses a unit of time, not a creature or person. Time itself receives divine blessing.

wayyĕqaddêš (ויקדש) — and he made it holy — the root qādaš (קדש) appears here for the first time in the Bible. Holy means set apart, consecrated, designated for a special purpose. The seventh day is the first holy thing in Scripture.

bārāʾ ʾĕlōhîm laʿăśôṯ (ברא אלהים לעשות) — God created to do — the final clause of verse 3 is compressed and difficult. Literally: that he ceased from all his work which God created to do. The Sabbath rest is embedded within the creative project itself.

verses.esv: These are the generations of the heavens and the earth when they were created, in the day that the LORD God made the earth and the heavens.
insights: tôlĕdôṯ (תולדות) — generations, account — this word introduces a new section in Genesis. It appears 10 times in Genesis, always as a section-heading formula introducing the descendants or account of a named party. Chapter 2 is 'the account of the heavens and the earth.'

YHWH ʾĕlōhîm (יהוה אלהים) — the LORD God — the divine name YHWH appears here for the first time in the Bible, combined with ʾĕlōhîm. The personal covenant name joins the generic name for God. This compound name dominates the garden narrative of chapters 2–3.

bĕhibbārĕʾām (בהבראם) — when they were created — a Niphal infinitive of bārāʾ, meaning in their being created. The same root that opened chapter 1 (bārāʾ) appears here in its passive form, using a wordplay in the consonants on ʾAḇrāhām (Abraham).

verses.esv: When no bush of the field was yet in the land and no small plant of the field had yet sprung up—for the LORD God had not yet caused it to rain on the land, and there was no man to work the ground,
insights: śîaḥ (שיח) — bush, shrub — a word referring to wild desert scrub or bush plants, not cultivated vegetation. The word appears only here and in Genesis 21:15 (where Hagar places her son under a bush). This is uncultivated wilderness vegetation.

ʿāḏām ʾayin (ואדם אין) — and there was no human — the absence of humanity is given as a reason why vegetation had not appeared. Humanity's absence is causally connected to agricultural barrenness. Humans are needed for the ground to function.

himṭîr (המטיר) — had caused to rain — the Hiphil causative: YHWH God caused it to rain. The same causative form appears in descriptions of the flood (7:4) and fire on Sodom (19:24). God is always the active causer of rain in the Hebrew Bible.

verses.esv: and a mist was going up from the land and was watering the whole face of the ground—
insights: ʾêḏ (ואד) — mist, stream — a rare word (only here and Job 36:27) whose exact meaning is debated. Ancient versions differ: LXX reads 'spring,' Vulgate 'spring/fountain,' Syriac 'cloud.' Most modern scholars favor 'stream' or 'flood' based on cognate Sumerian/Akkadian idu.

yaʿăleh (יעלה) — was going up — the watering of the ground from below, ascending mist or stream, contrasts with the rain from above (v.5). The pre-rain order of the world is irrigated from below. This bottom-up watering is the original order before sky-water was established.

kāl-pĕnê hāʾădāmāh (כל פני האדמה) — the whole face of the ground — the word ʾădāmāh (ground, soil) is the basis for ʾādām (human). The ground that produces humans is the same ground they are called to cultivate. The connection is etymological and vocational.

verses.esv: then the LORD God formed the man of dust from the ground and breathed into his nostrils the breath of life, and the man became a living creature.
insights: wayyîṣer (ויצר) — and he formed — from yāṣar (יצר), the verb of a potter shaping clay. BDB notes this root is used of a craftsman working with hands on material. God forms the human the way a potter forms clay. The same verb appears in Isaiah 45:9 — can the clay say to the potter, why are you forming me?

ʿāp̄ār (עפר) — dust — the specific word for loose dry soil, dirt, or dust. Not the cultivated earth (ʾădāmāh) or the cosmic earth (ʾereṣ) but fine, dry, elementary matter. Dust returns to dust (Genesis 3:19) — the human's origin and destination are the same substance.

nišmat ḥayyîm (נשמת חיים) — the breath of life — nĕšāmāh is a different word than rûaḥ (spirit/wind/breath of God in 1:2). nĕšāmāh is the breath within a creature's nostrils — the intimate interior breath. God breathes personally into the human's nostrils. This is the most intimate of all creation acts.

verses.esv: And the LORD God planted a garden in Eden, in the east, and there he put the man whom he had formed.
insights: gan (גן) — garden — the word for an enclosed, cultivated plot. The Garden of Eden is not wilderness; it is a walled or hedged cultivation, a garden-sanctuary. The same word describes the orchard in Song of Songs and the garden tomb in John 19–20.

bĕʿêḏen (בעדן) — in Eden — Eden as a place name means delight or pleasure in Hebrew. Scholars debate whether Eden is a geographic location or a symbolic name. The ancient versions vary: the LXX transliterates Eden; the Vulgate translates it as 'of pleasure.'

miqqeḏem (מקדם) — in the east — eastward is the direction of sunrise, of the sacred in ancient Near Eastern cosmology. The sanctuary-garden is planted where the sun rises. Later, Israel's tabernacle and temple face east, and the high priest enters from the east.

verses.esv: And out of the ground the LORD God made to spring up every tree that is pleasant to the sight and good for food. The tree of life was in the midst of the garden, and the tree of the knowledge of good and evil.
insights: neḥmāḏ (נחמד) — pleasant, desirable — from the root ḥāmaḏ (to desire, covet). The trees are described in the language of desire. The same root appears in the tenth commandment (do not covet) and in Genesis 3:6 when the woman 'saw that the tree was desirable.' Desire is woven into the garden.

ʿêṣ haḥayyîm (עץ החיים) — the tree of life — appears here in the middle of the garden, and again at the end of Genesis (3:22, 24). It resurfaces at the end of Revelation (22:2, 14, 19). The tree of life is the beginning and end of the biblical canon's narrative arc.

ʿêṣ hadaʿaṯ ṭôḇ wārāʿ (עץ הדעת טוב ורע) — the tree of the knowledge of good and evil — daʿaṯ means knowledge, intimacy, experience — the full experiential knowledge of a thing. The tree offers comprehensive experiential knowledge of the entire moral range. It is a dangerous gift.

verses.esv: A river flowed out of Eden to water the garden, and there it divided and became four rivers.
insights: nāhār (ונהר) — and a river — the singular river from Eden that waters the garden then divides. The geography is of a source flowing from the divine garden outward to the whole world. Eden is the fountainhead of all the world's waters.

yippārêḏ (יפרד) — divided — from the root pāraḏ (to separate, divide). The river divides into four heads. The same root is used of Lot and Abraham separating (Genesis 13:9) and in Ruth 1:17 (only death shall separate). Division from the divine source is a recurring theme.

ʾarḇāʿāh rāšîm (לארבעה ראשים) — four heads — rāʾš means head, chief, top, beginning. The heads of rivers are their sources or main streams. Four is the number of the world's directions, suggesting these four rivers encompass the whole world from the single divine garden source.

verses.esv: The name of the first is the Pishon. It is the one that flowed around the whole land of Havilah, where there is gold.
insights: pîšôn (פישון) — Pishon — the first of the four rivers. The name may derive from a root meaning to spring forth or leap. The Pishon river remains geographically unidentified by modern scholarship, though proposals range from the Indus to the Nile to the Arabian Peninsula.

haḥăwîlāh (החוילה) — Havilah — a land with gold, bdellium, and onyx. Havilah appears in Genesis 10:7, 29 and 25:18 in various genealogical contexts. The land's identification is also uncertain; some scholars propose Arabia or India.

hazzāhāḇ (הזהב) — the gold — Genesis presents Eden's geography as fabulously wealthy. Gold, bdellium, and onyx are the most valuable materials of the ancient world. The garden narrative begins in a landscape of abundance before the vocabulary of scarcity and toil in Genesis 3.

verses.esv: And the gold of that land is good; bdellium and onyx stone are there.
insights: ṭôḇ (טוב) — good — the first use of the theological key-word good since Genesis 1:31 (very good). Here it evaluates not God's creation but a material: the gold of that land is good. Quality, purity, and desirability are all contained in ṭôḇ.

habběḏōlaḥ (הבדלח) — the bdellium — a precious resin from trees, traded across the ancient world, white and fragrant like manna (Numbers 11:7). Its exact identification is debated; some propose a translucent mineral, others a fragrant plant resin.

ʾeḇen haššōham (ואבן השהם) — and the stone of onyx — šōham appears in the priestly garments of Exodus 28 as the stones on the high priest's shoulders, engraved with the names of the twelve tribes. What began as a precious mineral in Eden's geography becomes the substrate of Israel's priestly identity.

verses.esv: The name of the second river is the Gihon. It is the one that flowed around the whole land of Cush.
insights: gîḥôn (גיחון) — Gihon — means the bursting forth or gusher, from the root gûaḥ (to burst out). The same name is given to the spring of Gihon outside Jerusalem, the water source for the city of David. Connecting the garden river to Jerusalem's spring is significant.

kûš (כוש) — Cush — the land associated with the Nile regions south of Egypt; modern scholarship identifies this with Ethiopia or Sudan. The Gihon flowing around Cush would place it in the Nile valley region, though complete geographic identification remains uncertain.

The Gihon, as the spring that waters Jerusalem, may be the text's way of connecting the primordial garden sanctuary to the eventual sanctuary site of Zion. Eden–Jerusalem–New Jerusalem is a recurring symbolic trajectory in the Hebrew Bible.

verses.esv: And the name of the third river is the Tigris, which flows east of Assyria. And the fourth river is the Euphrates.
insights: ḥiddeqel (חדקל) — Hiddekel/Tigris — the Tigris River is explicitly named and geographically located east of Assyria (Ashur). This is the first firm geographic identification in the four-river list, anchoring the description in real ancient Near Eastern geography.

pĕrāṯ (פרת) — the Euphrates — mentioned simply by name, without qualification or description. The Euphrates is so well known it needs no explanation. It is the great river, the river par excellence of the ancient Fertile Crescent and Mesopotamian civilization.

The four rivers — Pishon, Gihon, Tigris, Euphrates — span the entire known world of the ancient author: from the unknown east (Pishon/Havilah) to Africa (Gihon/Cush) to Mesopotamia (Tigris and Euphrates). Eden is at the geographic center of everything.

verses.esv: The LORD God took the man and put him in the garden of Eden to work it and keep it.
insights: wayyiqqaḥ (ויקח) — and he took — the verb lāqaḥ (לקח) — to take. God personally takes the human and places him in the garden. The same verb will be used when God takes a rib from the human (2:21, 22). God's personal involvement in placement is emphasized.

lĕʿāḇĕḏāh ûlĕšāmĕrāh (לעבדה ולשמרה) — to work it and keep it — two infinitives define the human vocation: ʿāḇaḏ (to serve, work, till) and šāmar (to keep, guard, observe). These two verbs are used together again in Numbers 3:7–8 and 18:7 for the Levitical duties in the tabernacle.

The human in the garden is functioning as a priest in a sanctuary. lĕʿāḇĕḏāh (to work it) parallels priestly service (ʿăḇoḏāh) and lĕšāmĕrāh (to keep it) parallels the duty to guard the sacred space. The garden is a temple and the human is its priest.

verses.esv: And the LORD God commanded the man, saying, "You may surely eat of every tree of the garden,
insights: wayyĕṣaw (ויצו) — and he commanded — from the root ṣāwāh (צוה), to command, charge. The appearance of this root marks the first divine command addressed to a human being. The sequence of Genesis 1–2 places divine command at the conclusion of garden placement.

ʾāḵōl tōḵêl (אכל תאכל) — you may surely eat — an infinitive absolute paired with an imperfect: literally eating you shall eat. This emphatic construction (infinitive absolute + finite verb) emphasizes the fullness of the permission. Freedom is the baseline condition of human existence.

mikkōl ʿêṣ-haggān (מכל עץ הגן) — from every tree of the garden — the permission is unrestricted generosity. Every tree is available. The single restriction (v.17) stands against the backdrop of unlimited abundance. The command framework begins with abundance, not restriction.

verses.esv: but of the tree of the knowledge of good and evil you shall not eat, for in the day that you eat of it you shall surely die.
insights: lōʾ tōḵal (לא תאכל) — you shall not eat — the single prohibition. Against the unlimited permission of verse 16, one tree is excluded. The restriction is not punitive but definitional: the human's freedom operates within a bounded space defined by the Creator.

môt tāmût (מות תמות) — you shall surely die — again the emphatic infinitive absolute: dying you shall die. The construction mirrors the permission (eating you shall eat / dying you shall die). Permission and prohibition are structurally parallel, giving them equal weight.

bĕyôm ʾăḵālĕḵā (ביום אכלך) — in the day that you eat — the conditional time clause. Death is linked to the specific act of eating. The day of eating becomes the day of dying. This introduces the tension that drives the narrative of Genesis 3.

verses.esv: Then the LORD God said, "It is not good that the man should be alone. I will make him a helper fit for him."
insights: lōʾ ṭôḇ (לא טוב) — it is not good — the first negative divine evaluation in Genesis. Six times in Genesis 1 God declared something good (ṭôḇ). Now, for the first time, God declares something not good. Human aloneness is the first thing God identifies as incomplete in creation.

ʿêzer (עזר) — helper — a word of strength and assistance. Crucially, this word is used elsewhere primarily of God himself: God is Israel's helper (Psalm 121:2, 124:8). The helper role is not subordinate but powerful. The woman is not a diminished human but a strong ally.

kĕneḡdô (כנגדו) — fit for him, corresponding to him — a compound meaning facing, opposite, corresponding. The helper is not identical to or inferior to the human but standing across from him — a mirror, a counterpart, a corresponding ally who faces and matches him.

verses.esv: Now out of the ground the LORD God had formed every beast of the field and every bird of the heavens and brought them to the man to see what he would call them. And whatever the man called every living creature, that was its name.
insights: wayyîṣer (ויצר) — and he had formed — the same verb as verse 7 (wayyîṣer...ʾeṯ hāʾādām). God forms both human and animals from the ground (min hāʾădāmāh). Animals and humans share the same origin: dust of the ground and the same divine shaping verb.

lirʾôṯ (לראות) — to see — God brings the animals to the human to see what he would call them. God's seeing in Genesis 1 evaluated creation; here God watches to see what the human will name. The naming scene is a test of human wisdom and relational knowledge.

nepĕš ḥayyāh (נפש חיה) — living creature — used of the animals in 1:20, 24 and now in 2:19 for whatever the human names. The naming formula confirms: whatever the human calls each living creature, that is its name. Naming is an exercise of rational and royal authority.

verses.esv: The man gave names to all livestock and to the birds of the heavens and to every beast of the field. But for Adam there was not found a helper fit for him.
insights: wayyiqrāʾ (ויקרא) — and he called/gave names — the naming exercise is now completed. The human named every livestock animal, every bird, and every field creature. The scope of the naming covers all of the animal categories from Genesis 1:24–25 exactly.

ûlĕʾāḏām (ולאדם) — but for Adam — this is possibly the first place where ʾāḏām functions as a personal name rather than a common noun (the human). The shift from hāʾāḏām (the man, with article) to ʾāḏām (Adam, without article) is subtle but potentially significant.

lōʾ māṣāʾ (לא מצא) — was not found — the passive or impersonal form: there was not found for Adam a helper corresponding to him. The animal parade through the naming exercise has revealed a gap — the human has no relational counterpart among the creatures.

verses.esv: So the LORD God caused a deep sleep to fall upon the man, and while he slept took one of his ribs and closed up its place with flesh.
insights: tardêmāh (תרדמה) — deep sleep — a divine heavy sleep, not ordinary slumber. The same word describes the sleep of Abram during the covenant-making ceremony (Genesis 15:12) and the sleep of Saul's camp (1 Samuel 26:12). Tardêmāh is a sleep of divine action, not rest.

ṣēlāʿ (צלע) — rib, side — the word means rib or, more broadly, side. In Exodus and 1 Kings, the same word describes the sides of the ark, the tabernacle, and the temple. The woman is built from the man's side — and the temple is built from sides. The woman and the temple share the same Hebrew architectural vocabulary.

wayyisgor bāśār taḥtennāh (ויסגר בשר תחתנה) — and closed up with flesh in its place — God seals the wound. The flesh-closing is a surgical metaphor: God is an intimate craftsman of the body. The same root sāgar (to close, shut) appears in the sealing of Noah's ark.

verses.esv: And the rib that the LORD God had taken from the man he made into a woman and brought her to the man.
insights: wayyiḇen (ויבן) — and he built — from the root bānāh (בנה), to build. God builds the woman. Construction vocabulary — the same word for building cities, temples, and houses — is applied to the creation of the woman. She is an architectural work of God.

ṣēlāʿ (הצלע) — the rib — the same word for the temple's side chambers (1 Kings 6:5–8). The woman built from the man's side and the temple built from its sides share one Hebrew word. Both sanctuary and woman emerge from an opening in the male form.

wayyĕḇiʾehā (ויביאה) — and he brought her — God personally escorts the woman to the man. The sequence is: God builds, then brings. The presentation of the woman to the man is a formal presentation — God is the host of the first meeting and the agent of the first marriage.

verses.esv: Then the man said, "This at last is bone of my bones and flesh of my flesh; she shall be called Woman, because she was taken out of Man."
insights: happe̊ am (הפעם) — this at last — an expression of relief and recognition. After the naming procession, the human's first spoken words in the Bible are words of joyful recognition: finally! This one! The first human speech is an exclamation of delight at the sight of the woman.

ʾiššāh...meʾîš (אשה...מאיש) — Woman...from Man — the Hebrew wordplay is precise: the man is ʾîš (איש) and the woman is ʾiššāh (אשה). The two words sound like they share an etymology (though they probably don't). The text exploits the sound similarity to declare: she is from him, she corresponds to him.

ʾeṣem mêʾăṣāmay ûḇāśār mibběśārî (עצם מעצמי ובשר מבשרי) — bone of my bones and flesh of my flesh — the kinship formula of the ancient Near East. To say bone of my bone is to declare full kinship and solidarity. The same phrase appears in 2 Samuel 5:1 and 19:13 in political loyalty oaths.

verses.esv: Therefore a man shall leave his father and his mother and hold fast to his wife, and they shall become one flesh.
insights: wayyaʿăzāḇ (יעזב) — shall leave — from ʿāzaḇ (עזב), to leave, forsake, abandon. The man's primary relational loyalty shifts from parents to wife. The same verb is used of Israel forsaking God (Jeremiah 2:13) and of God promising never to forsake Israel (Isaiah 42:16). Covenant fidelity and marriage share the same vocabulary.

wĕdāḇaq (ודבק) — and hold fast — from dāḇaq (דבק), to cling, cleave, stick. BDB documents the root as describing inseparable attachment. The same verb describes Ruth's attachment to Naomi (1:14) and Israel's commanded attachment to God (Deuteronomy 10:20; 11:22; 13:4). Marital clinging is covenant clinging.

lĕḇāśār ʾeḥāḏ (לבשר אחד) — one flesh — the goal of leave and cleave is one flesh. This phrase describes marital union in physical, relational, legal, and theological dimensions. Jesus cites this verse (Matthew 19:5) as the foundation of the indissolubility of marriage.

verses.esv: And the man and his wife were both naked and were not ashamed.
insights: ʿărûmmîm (ערומים) — naked — the plural of ʿārôm (naked, bare). Genesis 2 ends with nakedness and no shame. Genesis 3:1 opens with the serpent described as ʿārûm (crafty, shrewd) — the same consonants, different vowels. The wordplay is deliberate: the naked without shame of Eden becomes the wily and shrewd of temptation.

lōʾ yiṯbōšāšû (לא יתבששו) — and were not ashamed — the Hitpael of bôš (to be ashamed, confused, put to shame). This reflexive-intensive form implies: they did not regard themselves with shame, they had no internal experience of shame in relation to each other. Shame comes only in Genesis 3:7.

Genesis 2:25 is often read as the definition of Eden-innocence: nakedness without shame. This is the pre-fall human condition — complete openness and vulnerability before God and each other, with no need for concealment. The contrast with Genesis 3:7 (they sewed fig leaves) is total and theologically critical.
